2025-04-18.thesis: China rare-earth export curbs; MP is sole scaled US producer
2025-04-18.exit_case: MP already surged hard on announcement; initial rare-earth panic bid may be fully priced in week one.
2025-04-18.assessment: First journal entry. China imposed rare-earth export controls (seven heavy rare-earth categories requiring export licenses) in early April 2025 as a trade-war escalation tool. The curbs have not been lifted; no reversal signal in this week's news. MP Materials remains the only scaled US miner/processor, making it the cleanest direct-equity vehicle. The White House tariff carve-outs mentioned in Australian press this week apply to electronics, not rare earths—so no catalyst bleed. The risk is that the move is front-loaded in price, but the catalyst itself (curbs in force, no export licenses flowing) is fully unresolved. Thesis intact; monitor for any MOFCOM license-approval announcements or bilateral trade communiqués that include rare-earth normalisation language.
2025-04-18.exit_advice: Hold MP while China export curbs remain formally in force. Exit trigger: China officially lifts/waives rare-earth export restrictions, or a US-China trade framework explicitly carves out rare earths and curbs are suspended.

2025-04-25.thesis: China rare-earth export curbs; MP is sole scaled US producer
2025-04-25.exit_case: Three-week-old curbs are fully public and priced; no new license-denial catalysts arrived this week to sustain momentum.
2025-04-25.assessment: This week's news flow is entirely orthogonal to rare-earth export controls (pharmaceutical tariffs, nuclear equities, general volatility commentary). The specific catalyst — China's seven-category heavy rare-earth export-licence requirement — is neither reversed nor softened. The front-loading risk flagged last week is the live concern: MP has had time to reprice and incremental newsflow is thin, which is what the exit_case captures. But the structural asymmetry persists: the restriction is still law, no licenses are flowing, and MP remains the only scaled US alternative. Nothing this week changes the thesis. Watch for MOFCOM statements or any US-China trade framework that explicitly covers critical minerals.
2025-04-25.exit_advice: Hold. The curbs remain formally in force — no MOFCOM license approvals, no bilateral communiqué, no rollback language surfaced in this week's news. Exit trigger: any credible signal that China has resumed issuing export licenses or that rare earths are included in a US-China trade normalisation package.

2025-05-02.thesis: China rare-earth export curbs; MP is sole scaled US producer
2025-05-02.exit_case: US GDP contraction (-0.3% Q1) signals demand slowdown; industrial rare-earth premium may compress with macro weakness.
2025-05-02.assessment: Third consecutive week with zero movement on the specific catalyst. China's seven-category heavy rare-earth export-licence requirement remains in force; no licenses are flowing and no bilateral communiqué covering critical minerals has appeared. The 'China holds composure in trade war / no new stimulus' headlines this week actually reinforce the thesis: Beijing is deliberately maintaining its leverage toolkit (of which rare-earth curbs are a pillar) rather than signalling any concession. The new incremental risk is the US GDP print (-0.3% Q1 2025), which is a genuine macro headwind — a slowing industrial economy can suppress rare-earth demand and compress commodity-equity premiums even if the export restriction stays on the books. This does not resolve the catalyst but it is the freshest challenge to the trade's risk/reward. The front-loading concern flagged since week 1 compound with this. Still, the structural asymmetry — curbs in force, no US alternative to MP at scale — is intact. MP remains the cleanest vehicle. Monitor: any MOFCOM licence-approval announcement, US-China trade framework language touching critical minerals, or MP-specific demand guidance.
2025-05-02.exit_advice: Hold; exit only on MOFCOM license approvals, explicit US-China critical-minerals carve-out, or confirmed GDP-led demand destruction in rare-earth end-markets.

2025-05-09.thesis: China rare-earth export curbs; MP is sole scaled US producer

The US-UK tariff deal (May 8) is the most relevant macro signal: it confirms the White House is actively deal-making, which raises the tail risk of a US-China framework that includes rare-earth concessions as a bargaining chip. That is the sharpest new threat to the thesis this week and reinforces the exit case. Separately, China's PBOC rate cut and consumption stimulus signal Beijing is cushioning its own economy — but nothing in that announcement touches export controls, which remain a deliberate leverage tool, not a growth policy. The structural asymmetry (curbs in force, no scaled US alternative to MP) is intact. The compounding risk remains: four weeks of re-pricing with no fresh catalyst newsflow is the classic stale-trade trap. The thesis is alive but the urgency to monitor US-China trade channel language is now higher than any prior week.
2025-05-09.exit_advice: Hold. Catalyst unresolved: no MOFCOM licence approvals, no US-China communiqué touching critical minerals. Exit trigger remains a confirmed reversal of the seven-category export-licence requirement or explicit bilateral rare-earth normalisation language. Monitor the US-UK deal as a template — if a US-China framework emerges that explicitly covers critical minerals, exit immediately.

2025-05-23.thesis: Trump nuclear executive action driving US reactor operator demand
2025-05-23.exit_case: EOs already signed/announced; 'explosive gains' in nuclear ETFs signal catalyst may be fully priced right at entry.
2025-05-23.assessment: Week 1. Trump's nuclear executive orders fired the starter gun on a uranium frenzy, with nuclear ETFs posting explosive gains and CEG named prominently. The executive actions themselves are at or near signing, but the downstream business impact for a reactor operator like CEG — streamlined NRC licensing, extended plant lifetimes, new data-centre/AI offtake contracts underwritten by regulatory certainty — is still weeks-to-months from translating into earnings. CEG is the cleanest vehicle: largest US nuclear fleet, pure reactor-operator exposure, and the direct institutional counterpart to any power-purchase demand unleashed by executive policy. Uranium miners and ETFs have front-run the news; CEG's fundamental re-rating (on actual capacity and contract announcements) is the durable second leg. Watch NRC rulemaking timelines and CEG management commentary on new customer commitments as the key confirmation signals.
2025-05-23.exit_advice: Exit if no concrete follow-through — NRC rulemaking, new CEG power-purchase agreements, or capacity additions — materialises within 4-6 weeks, or if CEG reverses sharply after the initial frenzy fades.

Posture: defensive hold through the Geneva expiry resolution only; exit on any bilateral critical-minerals language regardless of price direction.

Geneva truce expiry (~Aug 10) is the final identifiable binary — if it passes without rare-earth action, the thesis is indefinitely deferred and practical edge is nil.

2025-08-15.thesis: China rare-earth export curbs; MP is sole scaled US producer
2025-08-15.exit_case: Geneva 90-day truce expiry window (~Aug 10-12) passed with zero rare-earth action, eliminating the last identifiable near-term catalyst binary.
2025-08-15.assessment: Week 18; this week's news (Trump-Putin Alaska summit, Ukraine, DEI rulings, WWII anniversary) is entirely orthogonal — zero rare-earth signal for the eighteenth consecutive week. The single most important development is what is now a completed data point rather than a pending event: the Geneva 90-day tariff truce expiry (~Aug 10-12), flagged last week as the highest-stakes inflection point in the entire journal arc, passed without any rare-earth action in either direction — no MOFCOM licence approvals, no bilateral critical-minerals concession language, no re-escalation. China's seven-category heavy rare-earth export-licence requirement remains technically in force; catalyst_resolved is still false and MP remains the only scaled-US-producer vehicle. But the practical state of the trade could not be weaker: maximum diplomatic pressure (Geneva truce expiry) arrived and departed without touching rare earths, which removes the one remaining mechanism that could have injected fresh catalyst energy into an 18-week-stale position. There is now no identifiable near-term binary event on the horizon. The thesis is alive in the narrowest technical sense only — the curbs have not been formally reversed — but 18 consecutive weeks of zero incremental newsflow, the exhaustion of every flagged inflection point (Geneva truce, July 9 pause expiry, diplomatic 'positivity' signals, Geneva expiry), and the absence of any successor catalyst binary leave practical edge at or below zero. Hold posture is purely custodial: wait for the binary to resolve formally in one direction, with asymmetric alertness to a diplomatic concession as the most probable resolution path given the advancing US-China engagement channel.
2025-08-15.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any bilateral critical-minerals language, MOFCOM licence approval, or formal rare-earth concession framing in US-China communiqués — regardless of MP's price direction. The last flagged inflection point has now passed without yielding anything; no successor binary event is visible.
